message.content:

content.text: Let me start by reading the current state of the test and implementation files.

I can see the current state. There are 9 passing tests and one `it.todo()` remaining. Now I'll activate the test for the stable block pattern.

Step 1: Activate the test.

The 2x2 block (stable pattern) consists of cells "0,0", "1,0", "0,1", "1,1". Each cell has exactly 3 live neighbors, so all survive and no new cells are born. The next generation should equal the same set.

content.text:
The test passed immediately - all 10 tests are green. This is an interesting outcome that requires explanation.

The prediction was that there would be no compilation error (correct), but I predicted there would be a runtime/assertion error. Instead, the test passed immediately because the existing implementation already correctly handles the 2x2 block pattern.

Let me reason through why: each cell in the 2x2 block ("0,0", "1,0", "0,1", "1,1") has exactly 3 live neighbors. The survival rule requires 2 or 3 live neighbors, so all 4 cells survive. No dead cell adjacent to the block has exactly 3 live neighbors (each corner-adjacent dead cell has exactly 1 live neighbor, each edge-adjacent dead cell has 2 live neighbors). So the implementation correctly returns the same set.

Red Phase Complete:
**Test Activated**: "should handle a stable block pattern (2x2 square survives unchanged)"
**Prediction**: No compilation error (correct). Runtime error prediction: the implementation was expected to fail, but it already handles this case correctly because each cell in the 2x2 block has exactly 3 live neighbors (satisfying the survival condition) and no dead adjacent cell has exactly 3 live neighbors (so no new births occur). The test passed on first run.
**Result**: All 10 tests pass. No Red phase failure occurred - the existing implementation already satisfied this test.

This is a valid TDD outcome: when activating a test reveals the implementation already handles the case, the test goes directly to green. This can happen when prior tests drove out a general enough solution.
